Raycast Extensions I Actually Use — Level Up Your Raycast

A while back a friend watched me open projects and run through my workflow on a Mac and asked if I had some secret stash of useful Raycast extensions. So here's a rundown of the Raycast extensions I actually use — plus aliases for each.
I've written about Raycast itself before, so I'll skip the intro — if you're new to Raycast, check out my earlier post:
Replace Mac's Built-In Spotlight With Raycast
Each entry below has a quick description, the download link, and the alias I use.
Any frontend dev knows TinyPNG — it's been the go-to image compressor forever. This Raycast extension brings TinyPNG into Raycast so you can compress images right from your launcher.
You'll need a TinyPNG token first. I've written about how to grab one in this post —
Uploading files to Firebase Storage with Node.js, including the TinyPNG section (article in Mandarin)
Just read the "壓縮圖片 (TinyPNG)" section.
The free tier gets you 500 compressions per month — plenty for normal use.
A Raycast extension that surfaces and kills processes on your Mac. If you've ever hit "Mac Port xxx is already in use", this thing is for you — find the stuck process and kill it with one keystroke.
The one I use 80%+ of my day. Instead of digging through Finder to find a project folder and then opening VSCode, just search the project name in Raycast and hit Enter.
I'm too lazy to open a browser tab and search "Google Translate" every time I need to translate something. This extension brings it into Raycast.
Internet feeling slow? Not sure if it's your ISP or just everything else? Usually you'd open a browser, search "Speedtest", click through — this extension just runs Speedtest directly from Raycast.
Frontend dev → JSON everywhere. Sometimes you get a wall of unformatted JSON and need it pretty-printed fast. This extension does that without leaving Raycast.
Before using this one — if you're not totally clear on Base64, I'd really recommend reading my
What Is Encryption? Why Do We Encrypt? A Beginner's Take
post first. Base64 is encoding, not encryption — know the difference before you reach for it.
The extension itself just turns text into Base64 (and back) without opening a browser tab.
JWT is everywhere as a token format, and it's reversible to inspect (since the payload isn't encrypted). If you want to know how JWT actually works,
Day 29 — JWT Authentication, Explained (article in Mandarin)
This extension takes a JWT and decodes it back to JSON so you can see what's inside.
That's the set — hopefully a few of these speed up your day too.

A friend asked why my project switching and dev workflow feels so snappy on Raycast — turns out it's mostly the extensions. This post is a rundown of the Raycast extensions I actually keep installed: TinyPNG, Kill Process, Visual Studio Code, Google Translate, Speedtest, Format JSON, Base64, and JWT Decoder — with my aliases for each.
